summary: Reis contrasts the skill expectations of pilots versus tech workers, noting that pilots reach a competency ceiling where they simply need to safely transport passengers, while tech workers face constant pressure to upgrade skills. He questions whether the '10x engineer' mythology makes sense when applied to other professions, concluding that being 'good enough' varies dramatically by field.
key_points: Pilots have a clear ceiling on job performance - safety and successful transport - beyond which additional skill doesn't add value
The '10x engineer' concept doesn't translate to other professions like aviation
Tech jobs require constantly pushing skills forward, while other jobs require retaining skills for rare events
Boredom and pay ceilings from hitting competency limits cause pilot burnout
money_quotes: It's not like you'll get a bonus for doing rolls in a full-passenger plane at 30,000 feet.

A pilot needs to have an arsenal of skills and nerves in the extremely rare case something happens. But they're not expected to be 10x'ing as a pilot every day. That would be weird and probably very concerning for passengers.
Here, we have two opposite expectations—one where you constantly reevaluate your skills to execute and deliver daily, and another where you retain your skills in case of rare events.
In both cases, assuming you're employed, you're good enough for your job by definition.
key_insight: The relentless 'level up or die' mentality of tech careers is an anomaly rather than the norm, and most professions simply require you to be reliably competent rather than exceptional.
